Home Reports

Cat's Got Your Files: Lynx Ransomware

Key Takeaways

  • The intrusion began with a successful RDP login using already-compromised credentials.
  • The threat actor moved laterally to a domain controller via RDP using a separate compromised domain admin account.
  • Sensitive files from multiple network shares were collected, compressed using 7-Zip, and exfiltrated via temp.sh.
  • The threat actor deployed Lynx ransomware across multiple backup and file servers via RDP.

Case Summary

The intrusion began in early March 2025 with a single successful Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) logon to an internet-exposed system. The threat actor authenticated using valid credentials and immediately began reconnaissance using cmd.exe and standard Windows utilities.

The actor used T1078 Valid Accounts for initial access and pivoted via T1021.001 Remote Desktop Protocol for lateral movement.

Initial Access

The first instance of unauthorized access was a successful RDP logon to the beachhead host using valid credentials. No evidence of brute force or password spraying was observed.

Execution

The threat actor used Windows Command Shell (cmd.exe) and PowerShell to execute commands during hands-on-keyboard activity. These tools were primarily used to run Living Off The Land Binaries (LOLBins).

Persistence

The threat actor created three look-alike domain accounts using dsa.msc and added them to privileged security groups. AnyDesk was also installed as a service on the domain controller.

Discovery

The threat actor performed extensive network and system discovery using SoftPerfect NetScan, ipconfig, route print, systeminfo, and the registry query reg query HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Virtual Machine\\Guest\\Parameters.

Lateral Movement

The threat actor used RDP extensively for lateral movement, connecting to domain controllers, hypervisors, and backup servers.

Collection

The threat actor collected several folders from multiple file servers, archiving them with 7-Zip via the explorer.exe context menu.

Command and Control

All activity was performed via RDP from IP addresses 195.211.190.189 and 77.90.153.30, both hosted on Railnet LLC infrastructure (a known bulletproof hosting front).

Exfiltration

The threat actor used temp.sh to exfiltrate compressed archives via web upload.

Impact

The threat actor deployed Lynx ransomware (w.exe) on multiple backup and file servers, executed with arguments --dir E:\\ --mode fast --verbose --noprint. The actor also removed backup jobs from the Veeam configuration database to inhibit recovery.

Diamond Model

Detections

Network

ET DROP Spamhaus DROP Listed Traffic Inbound group 7
ET DROP Spamhaus DROP Listed Traffic Inbound group 37
ET SCAN Behavioral Unusual Port 135 traffic Potential Scan or Infection

Sigma DFIR Rules:

8a0d153f-b4e4-4ea7-9335-892dfbe17221 : NetScan Share Enumeration Write Access Check
53ad7638-3862-49a2-9ddd-af7132f9e598 : Using Netscan for Post-Scanning Lateral Movement
b26feb0b-8891-4e66-b2e7-ec91dc045d58 : AnyDesk Network
d7838d77-3a28-45cd-bc0e-7b941b687a39 : HyperVisor Discovery Activity

Yara

MAL_RANSOM_INC_Aug24

MITRE ATT&CK

Additional Local or Domain Groups - T1098.007
Archive via Utility - T1560.001
Data Encrypted for Impact - T1486
Domain Account - T1136.002
Exfiltration Over Web Service - T1567
External Remote Services - T1133
Inhibit System Recovery - T1490
Local Account - T1087.001
Network Service Discovery - T1046
Network Share Discovery - T1135
PowerShell - T1059.001
Query Registry - T1012
Remote Access Software - T1219
Remote Desktop Protocol - T1021.001
Remote System Discovery - T1018
System Information Discovery - T1082
System Network Configuration Discovery - T1016
Valid Accounts - T1078
Windows Command Shell - T1059.003
Windows Service - T1543.003
